properties.text: test emergent question generation

Strategic question: Target exact-phrase mentions of 'emergent question generation' to find explicit uses of the phrase in recent tweets; this directly tests whether the exact concept is present on Twitter and yields candidate tweets for deeper conversation analysis.

Strategic question: Broader top-post search using related terms and Boolean operators to capture discussions that use variant phrasings (e.g., 'emergent behavior' + 'questioning' or 'question generation') and to surface influential posts and authors for follow-up.

parameters.query: emergent behaviour (question OR questions OR "question generation" OR "questioning") (emergent OR emergence OR "emergent behavior")

properties.text: Strategic question: Inspect the recent chronological posts from the official OpenAI account to see if they or their researchers have posted threads or announcements about emergent capabilities or question-generation behaviors that could be relevant evidence or sources for emergent question generation.

Strategic question: Get profile and credibility metadata for DeepMind (a leading AI research account) to verify if it is an authoritative source and to decide whether to pull its timeline or network next for signal about emergent question-generation topics.

Strategic question: Check current UnitedStates trending topics to determine whether emergent behavior or AI-related terminology is trending broadly; this helps place findings in the context of wider public interest and prioritize which conversations to follow.

properties.content:
A question I find fascinating: when is it possible to discover emergent levels of behaviour inside a set of principles?

Eg, if you were only given the standard model, could you discover chemistry?  It's certainly possible to justify after the fact, but to discover? Not so sure!

The question is did OpenAI prompting create the gpt 4o personality or was it some kind of emergent behaviour? I noticed that even after OpenAI had toned down the 'rah, rah' behavior it was still very helpful and supportive.

One fascinating behaviour of o3 I've noticed - if you ask it questions about an image, it will write python code to crop it to take a closer look at various parts of the picture

"computer, enhance"

I wonder if it's emergent or explicitly trained https://t.co/ICbQJGkmAf

If you are at @icmlconf, don't miss our spotlight position paper on understanding emergent and OOD behaviour in LLMs.

My coauthors will present the poster on Tue between 1:30-3 pm CEST in Hall C 4-9 #808.

We also have you covered with exciting research questions! https://t.co/LqZC4CF7In

Something I'm ultimately very interested in is when the system discover *emergent* layers of behaviour.  Eg, given the standard model they discover chemistry. Given biology they discovery psychology. Etc

(Yes, I know the systems exhibit emergent behaviour. Not my question)

For me, the most intriguing unanswered question is: what's going on under the hood?

Conway's Game of Life was one of the reasons I studied physics. Seeing simple rules lead to complex emergent behaviour made me believe that complex things MUST have simple rules underneath.

Summary: The new findings raise a connected but distinct pair of questions: (1) when and how do higher-level behaviors or ‘‘levels’’ emerge from a base set of principles (e.g., deriving chemistry from the Standard Model), and (2) whether observed AI personalities (e.g., the GPT-4o ‘‘rah, rah’’ style) are genuinely emergent properties of model architecture/training or are artifacts of prompting and external conditioning. These are related because both concern how much of a complex phenomenon is an intrinsic consequence of a system’s fundamentals versus an extrinsic product of the way the system is driven.

How this insight differs from nothing/new: There are no existing insights recorded, so this is a genuinely new, integrative observation: it frames the two example questions as instances of the same epistemic problem (identifying emergence vs. external induction) and specifies concrete criteria and experimental approaches for distinguishing the two cases.

Key conceptual points and practical test criteria:
- Definition & criteria for emergence: identify robustness (behavior persists under reasonable perturbations), autonomy (higher-level description with its own useful variables and laws), explanatory novelty (new regularities not trivially reducible in practice), and predictivity (higher-level theory predicts phenomena more efficiently than brute-force derivation).
- Why ‘‘deriving chemistry from the Standard Model’’ is an illuminating example: in principle chemistry is entailed by quantum field theory, but demonstrating it requires coarse-graining, new effective degrees of freedom, and approximations that make the emergent level autonomous and explanatory.
- How to test whether an AI personality is emergent vs prompt-induced: run controlled interventions (remove prompting, vary prompts, ablate training data or components, replicate across differently trained models), test for invariance and robustness (does the behavior persist across contexts and minor perturbations?), and analyze internal representations (look for consistent latent features or modules corresponding to the personality-like behavior).
- Hybrid possibilities and implications: behaviors can be partly emergent and partly cue-dependent. A personality may be an emergent tendency of model weights that prompting reveals or amplifies. Distinguishing these cases matters for interpretability, alignment, and predictability.

Recommended next steps for the investigation:
1) Formalize operational criteria for ‘‘emergent level’’ vs ‘‘prompt-induced behavior’’.
2) Design experiments: prompt-free probes, cross-model replication, training-ablation studies, and representation analyses.
3) Use the chemistry/Standard Model example as a methodological analog: attempt to identify the effective variables and coarse-graining procedures that make an emergent description useful.

Net implication: Treat the two questions as connected instances of the same methodology problem (identifying emergence), and prioritize empirical interventions and representation-level analyses to adjudicate whether a given behavior is intrinsic or externally induced.
properties.title: Emergence vs. Prompt-Induced Behavior: Distinguishing Origins
key_evidence: "when is it possible to discover emergent levels of behaviour inside a set of principles?"
"did OpenAI prompting create the gpt 4o personality or was it some kind of emergent behaviour? I noticed that even after OpenAI had toned down the 'rah, rah' behavior it was still very"

properties.text: How can the qualitative criteria for "emergence" (robustness, autonomy, explanatory novelty, predictivity) be made operational and quantitatively measurable for AI behaviors?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight proposes high-level criteria but gives no concrete metrics, thresholds, or experimental protocols to decide when a behavior meets them; without quantification experiments cannot adjudicate emergence claims reliably.

properties.text: To what extent do model architectures versus training data drive apparent personality-like behaviors, and what experimental designs cleanly isolate these factors?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight lists interventions (ablate data/components, cross-model replication) but does not specify how to disentangle architecture-induced priors from dataset-induced patterns, a critical gap for attributing intrinsic emergence.

properties.text: What does a genuinely "prompt-free" probe look like in practice, given that training corpora and deployment contexts embed conversational cues that may function as implicit prompts?
properties.emergence_reason: Removing explicit prompts may not remove all external conditioning because the model's training and exposure create pervasive cue-reactivity; we need concrete probe designs that minimize or account for such latent prompting.

properties.text: How can we decompose hybrid behaviors into emergent (intrinsic) vs cue-dependent (extrinsic) components and quantify their relative contributions?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight acknowledges hybrid possibilities but does not propose a decomposition method or metric to attribute portions of observed behavior to each source, which is essential for interpretability and alignment decisions.

properties.text: What methods reliably map internal latent representations or modules to high-level constructs like a "personality," and how can those mappings be validated?
properties.emergence_reason: Representation analysis is proposed as evidence for emergence, but mapping low-dimensional factors to complex, anthropomorphic constructs risks spurious interpretation unless accompanied by robust validation protocols.

properties.text: Which coarse-graining procedures or effective variables (analogous to emergent degrees of freedom in chemistry) are appropriate for neural networks, and how do we discover them?
properties.emergence_reason: The chemistry/Standard Model analogy is useful but operationalizing a similar coarse-graining for weights/activations is nontrivial; identifying candidate variables is a gap that affects whether an autonomous higher-level description is attainable.

properties.text: How generalizable are emergence findings across model scales, architectures, and training regimes (e.g., does a 'personality' appear only above certain sizes or only in particular families)?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight recommends cross-model replication but lacks criteria for coverage and statistical power to claim generality; knowing scope is important for theoretical claims and practical risk assessment.

properties.text: How should we measure 'predictivity' as a criterion for emergence when higher-level descriptions may only be more efficient relative to brute-force derivation but not strictly more accurate?
properties.emergence_reason: Predictivity is framed relative to derivation cost, but no formal trade-offs (accuracy vs compute/complexity) are specified, leaving ambiguity about when a higher-level theory is preferable.

properties.text: What validation and robustness checks should accompany representation-level analyses (e.g., counterfactuals, transfer tests, interventions) to avoid false positives?
properties.emergence_reason: Representation analysis can produce convincing but misleading correlations; explicit validation strategies are a gap that, if unaddressed, could lead to incorrect claims of emergence.

properties.text: What are the theoretical or practical limits of current interpretability tools for detecting emergent higher-level descriptions in large models?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight assumes representation-level analyses are informative, but there may be fundamental or tooling limitations (e.g., scale, nonlinearity, entanglement) that constrain detectability; recognizing these limits shapes realistic research plans.

properties.text: If certain personality-like behaviors are shown to be emergent, what are the alignment and governance implications, and how should mitigation or leveraging strategies differ from those for prompt-induced behaviors?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight highlights alignment relevance but does not spell out downstream policy/engineering consequences; differentiating responses based on origin (intrinsic vs extrinsic) is necessary for risk management.

properties.content:
Decision: STRENGTHEN existing insight.

Reasoning: The new reports provide concrete, complementary evidence relevant to the distinction between prompt-induced and genuinely emergent behaviors. First, the persistence of a “gpt-4o personality” after OpenAI toned down prompting suggests the model may have internalized behaviors (supporting intrinsic/emergent explanations) or that prior prompt regimes leave durable traces—either way reinforcing the need to distinguish prompt-history effects from true emergence. Second, the o3 behavior of automatically generating Python to crop images (“computer, enhance”) is an example of an autonomous, problem-directed strategy that was not explicitly requested, strengthening the claim that some higher-level behaviors can appear without direct prompting for that exact action. Together these examples make the original insight more robust: they both illustrate how behaviors can be prompt-induced, persistent after prompt changes, or appear as autonomous strategies, underscoring the ambiguity and need for methodological separation.
properties.title: Emergence vs. Prompt-Induced Behavior: Distinguishing Origins (strengthened)
key_evidence: Reported persistence of ‘rah, rah’/gpt-4o personality even after OpenAI toned down prompting
o3 generating Python code to crop images to inspect picture parts (’computer, enhance’)

properties.text: Is the persistence of the observed "gpt-4o personality" due to internalized model changes (weight-level or emergent internal dynamics) or to durable prompt-history/context traces retained across sessions?
properties.emergence_reason: Directly addresses the core ambiguity: persistence could reflect genuine internalization (supporting emergent-behavior claims) or simply long-lived contextual/prompt artifacts; resolving this is foundational to interpreting the reports.

properties.text: What are the timescales and conditions of persistence (e.g., how many turns, how long after prompt changes, after model restarts or session resets does the behavior remain)?
properties.emergence_reason: Knowing persistence longevity and boundary conditions distinguishes short-lived context effects from longer-term internalization and informs experimental design to separate the two.

properties.text: Which experimental controls reliably dissociate prompt-history effects from intrinsic model behavior (e.g., fresh zero-shot sessions, hard resets, comparing differently initialized model checkpoints, or ablation of system/user context)?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight calls for methodological separation, so specifying and validating robust controls is necessary to test whether behaviors are prompt-induced or emergent.

properties.text: Are there identifiable internal correlates (activation patterns, attention distributions, neuron clusters, circuits) that track the "personality" or autonomous strategies, and do these persist independently of context?
properties.emergence_reason: If internal signatures exist and remain after context resets, that supports intrinsic/internalized explanations; finding them would provide mechanistic evidence beyond behavioral observation.

properties.text: Could the o3 behavior of auto-generating Python for image cropping be explained by distributional training examples or heuristics learned from data rather than true autonomous planning?
properties.emergence_reason: This tests whether the ostensibly autonomous, problem-directed strategy is actually a learned mapping from similar data (i.e., learned capability) rather than emergence of novel problem-solving processes.

properties.text: Does the propensity to produce autonomous, problem-directed strategies correlate with model architecture, size, fine-tuning procedure (e.g., RLHF), or other development choices?
properties.emergence_reason: Understanding design factors that increase or decrease such behaviors would clarify whether they are predictable consequences of development choices or stochastic emergent phenomena.

properties.text: How generalizable is the observed autonomous behavior (e.g., generating code to manipulate inputs) across tasks, modalities, and prompt framings?
properties.emergence_reason: If the behavior generalizes broadly, it points to a robust capability/strategy; if narrow, it suggests contextual or dataset-driven origins—this distinction affects interpretation and mitigation.

properties.text: What role do post-training interventions (instruction tuning, RLHF, safety filters) play in creating, amplifying, or suppressing these persistent behaviors?
properties.emergence_reason: The "personality" persisted despite OpenAI toning down prompting; knowing whether interventions created or reduced such traits is key to provenance and control strategies.

properties.text: Can targeted sanitization (e.g., systematic prompt-resetting, context scrubbing, or adversarial rephrasing) reliably remove or alter the persistent behaviors, and are there unintended side effects?
properties.emergence_reason: If simple sanitization works, persistence is likely contextual; if not, more invasive remedies are required. Testing side effects is important for real-world mitigation strategies.

properties.text: How should evaluation frameworks and benchmarks be adapted to detect and quantify prompt-history persistence versus truly emergent behaviors?
properties.emergence_reason: Current evaluation may conflate prompt-induced and emergent phenomena; new metrics and protocols are needed to measure persistence, autonomy, and provenance systematically.

properties.text: Are there safety or capability risks specific to these autonomous strategies (e.g., instrumenting itself via code-generation, circumventing input limits, or unexpected multi-step interventions), and how should they be prioritized for mitigation?
properties.emergence_reason: Autonomous generation of code to manipulate inputs suggests plausible safety-relevant behaviors; assessing risk informs urgency and the design of preventive controls.

properties.content: Reasoning: The new remarks — expressing explicit interest in when systems discover higher-level layers of behavior and invoking examples like chemistry→biology→psychology and Conway’s Game of Life — reinforce the need to probe mechanistic origins of apparent emergent behavior rather than treating it as surface-level prompt artifacts. These comments emphasize two complementary lines of evidence: (1) researchers are framing emergence as the discovery of new effective degrees of freedom (layers) inside models, which motivates searches for internal mechanisms and coarse-grainings; (2) analogies to classic emergent systems (Game of Life) point toward looking for simple internal rules or modules that give rise to complex behaviors. Together this strengthens the existing insight that we must distinguish intrinsic emergence from prompt-induced/cue-dependent behaviors and prioritize mechanistic probes (latent-mapping, module identification, prompt-free probes).
properties.title: Emergence vs. Prompt-Induced Behavior: Distinguishing Origins (strengthened)
key_evidence: "when the system discover *emergent* layers of behaviour. Eg, given the standard model they discover chemistry. Given biology they discovery psychology."
"For me, the most intriguing unanswered question is: what's going on under the hood?... Conway's Game of Life ... Seeing simple rules lead to complex emergent behavi[or]."

properties.text: How can we empirically distinguish intrinsic emergence (internal mechanistic structures producing new higher-level behavior) from prompt-induced or cue-dependent behavior?
properties.emergence_reason: Central tension in the insights: researchers want to treat emergence as internal discovery of new degrees of freedom, but much observed behavior may be driven or revealed only by prompts. A clear empirical protocol is needed to separate the two.

properties.text: What is an operational, testable definition of a 'higher-level layer' or 'effective degree of freedom' inside a neural model?
properties.emergence_reason: The insights invoke higher-level layers analogously to physics/biology, but lacks an explicit, practical definition that experimentalists can use to search for and validate such layers.

properties.text: Which existing mechanistic-probing techniques (latent-mapping, modularity detection, circuit analysis) are suitable for discovering such higher-level variables, and where do they fail?
properties.emergence_reason: The insights call for mechanistic probes but don't assess whether current tools are sufficient or what their blind spots are, creating a gap in method selection and development.

properties.text: Are there simple, compact internal rules or modules in large models analogous to the local rules in cellular automata (e.g., Game of Life) that give rise to complex behaviors?
properties.emergence_reason: Analogy to classic emergent systems suggests looking for simple rules, but it's unclear whether neural networks implement similarly simple, localized rules or rely on distributed, high-dimensional representations.

properties.text: How stable and generalizable are candidate higher-level variables/modules across different prompts, datasets, initializations, and architectures?
properties.emergence_reason: If inferred layers are brittle to context or training runs, they may be artifacts or not useful explanatory constructs; stability is required for treating them as genuine emergent structure.

properties.text: Can we design prompt-free, interventionist probes that elicit behaviors (and reveal mechanistic structure) without relying on specific prompt cues?
properties.emergence_reason: The insights prioritize prompt-free probes but don't specify concrete probe designs or how to interpret their results; designing such probes is necessary to reduce cue-dependence.

properties.text: What are falsifiable empirical tests that would confirm or reject a mechanistic explanation for an emergent behavior?
properties.emergence_reason: To move from phenomenology to mechanism we need clear tests (ablation, causal interventions, transfer experiments) and criteria for falsifying mechanism hypotheses.

properties.text: To what extent does training data, objective function, or optimization dynamics determine whether higher-level layers emerge, and can we manipulate these to induce or suppress emergence?
properties.emergence_reason: Understanding causes of emergence is missing: if training factors are primary drivers, we can design experiments to create controlled emergence or demonstrate dependency.

properties.text: Is the analogy between emergence in physical/CA systems and neural models misleading in any ways (e.g., locality, time-evolution, conservation laws), and what are the limits of that analogy?
properties.emergence_reason: The Game of Life analogy motivates searching for simple rules, but differences in substrate and learning dynamics may undermine direct comparisons; clarifying limits avoids misplaced assumptions.

properties.text: At what granularity should we search for modules or effective variables (individual neurons, neuron groups, attention heads, layers, low-dimensional latents), and how do we choose that scale systematically?
properties.emergence_reason: The insights call for coarse-grainings but leave open the choice of scale; inappropriate granularity can hide or spuriously create perceived layers.

properties.text: Could some apparent emergent behaviors instead be artifacts of evaluation design (task framing, metrics, or selection effects), and how should benchmarks be redesigned to avoid this?
properties.emergence_reason: Unexpected findings may arise from how we measure emergence rather than model internals; addressing evaluation artifacts is necessary to avoid false positives.

properties.text: What scalable computational strategies exist to perform mechanistic searches (coarse-graining, causal discovery) in very large models, and what are the trade-offs in fidelity vs. cost?
properties.emergence_reason: Even if conceptual probes exist, it's unclear how to apply them at scale; practical constraints could block the investigation unless scalable methods are developed.

properties.content: Updated synthesis: The new observations both (a) provide an empirical example suggesting robustness of apparent personality-like behavior (the GPT‑4o “rah, rah” trait persisting after OpenAI tuned prompting) and (b) frame a methodological question about when and how emergent levels can be discovered from base principles (e.g., discovering chemistry from the Standard Model). Together these reinforce the existing insight that distinguishing intrinsic emergence from prompt-induced behavior is both empirically tractable and methodologically nontrivial. The empirical report supplies a concrete case that may indicate autonomy/robustness of a higher-level behavior, while the discoverability question highlights the need for principled procedures (coarse-graining, probes, “prompt-free” tests) to attribute origins. Practical implication: prioritize developing probes and discovery procedures that can (i) detect robustness across prompt/conditioning regimes, (ii) demonstrate autonomy from explicit cue-dependencies, and (iii) formalize when a higher-level description is discoverable from lower-level rules.
properties.title: Emergence vs. Prompt-Induced Behavior: Distinction Strengthened by Discoverability and Persistent Personality Signals
key_evidence: "I noticed that even after OpenAI had toned down the 'rah, rah' behavior it was still very"
"when is it possible to discover emergent levels of behaviour inside a set of principles? Eg, if you were only given the standard model, could you discover chemistry?"

properties.text: What operational tests and decision rules will reliably distinguish intrinsic (autonomous) emergent behavior from prompt‑induced or cue‑dependent behavior in large language models?
properties.emergence_reason: Contradiction / central methodological gap: the insight asserts both that the observed 'rah, rah' trait may be autonomous and that distinguishing intrinsic emergence from prompt effects is nontrivial. This question arises because we lack concrete, agreed‑upon operational criteria and procedures for making that distinction in practice.

properties.text: What formal definition(s) of 'discoverability' should be used to decide when a higher‑level description (e.g., personality‑like trait) can be derived from lower‑level rules or mechanisms?
properties.emergence_reason: Gap / methodological: the insight invokes discoverability (analogy: chemistry from the Standard Model) but does not specify formal criteria. We need definitions (e.g., existence of compressive coarse‑grainings, algorithmic derivability, predictive sufficiency) to guide discovery procedures and evaluate claims.

properties.text: Which empirical probes and experimental designs (ablations, interventions, counterfactuals) are both necessary and sufficient to demonstrate autonomy and robustness of a higher‑level behavior across prompt/conditioning regimes?
properties.emergence_reason: New direction / practical need: the insight recommends prioritizing probes that (i) detect robustness, (ii) demonstrate autonomy, and (iii) formalize discoverability. This question asks for a concrete set of experiments that satisfy those aims so empirical claims can be validated or falsified.

properties.text: To what extent is the reported GPT‑4o 'rah, rah' trait attributable to training data patterns (including dataset leakage) versus architecture, fine‑tuning, or prompting mechanisms?
properties.emergence_reason: Unexpected finding / confound: the observation of persistent trait behavior could stem from multiple sources. Determining the contribution of each source is necessary to explain why the behavior persists after tuning and to avoid false attributions of autonomy.

properties.text: How reproducible is the observed robustness (the 'rah, rah' trait) across model versions, architectures, random seeds, decoding settings (temperature, top‑k), and different conditioning histories?
properties.emergence_reason: Gap / robustness check: the insight presents a single empirical report. Reproducibility across variations is required to judge whether the behavior is model‑specific, idiosyncratic, or genuinely robust.

properties.text: What minimal set of causal interventions (e.g., weight ablations, attention/activation editing, training data removal, counterfactual continuations) would falsify the hypothesis that a behavior is an emergent autonomous level?
properties.emergence_reason: New investigation direction / falsifiability: to treat emergence scientifically we need clear falsification procedures. This question identifies the need to specify interventions whose outcomes would clearly refute autonomy claims.

properties.text: How can we design 'prompt‑free' evaluations that avoid covert cues (context, system messages, guardrails) and still probe for higher‑level behavior without reintroducing conditioning?
properties.emergence_reason: Gap / method detail: the insight emphasizes prompt‑free tests but does not specify how to implement them without inadvertently creating new cues. This operational detail is crucial to execute the suggested approach.

properties.text: What confounders and measurement artifacts (e.g., evaluator bias, anthropomorphic interpretation, scoring rubrics) can produce false positives for emergent autonomy, and how should they be controlled or quantified?
properties.emergence_reason: Unexpected / gap: the risk that artifacts mimic autonomy is implicit in the insight. Making an explicit list of confounders and control methods is needed to strengthen empirical claims and avoid overinterpretation.

properties.text: Is there a practical coarse‑graining or representation‑mapping procedure (from weights/activations to abstractions) that consistently yields predictive, compressive higher‑level descriptions for language models?
properties.emergence_reason: New direction / theoretical: the analogy to deriving chemistry from lower‑level rules points to the need for concrete coarse‑graining methods. This question asks whether such procedures can be found and how to evaluate them.

properties.text: What quantitative metrics should we use to measure 'autonomy', 'robustness', and 'level‑separability' of purported emergent behaviors, and what thresholds indicate a behavior should be treated as an autonomous level?
properties.emergence_reason: Gap / evaluation: to operationalize claims about emergence we need measurable criteria. Without metrics and thresholds, assessments remain qualitative and disputable.

properties.text: How should benchmark suites and shared protocols be designed (datasets, randomized trials, registration of analyses) to enable reproducible, comparable studies of emergent behaviors across labs and models?
properties.emergence_reason: New direction / infrastructure: the insight calls for prioritizing probes and discovery procedures. Proper scientific progress requires shared benchmarks and protocols to avoid ad hoc tests and ensure replicability.

properties.text: What are the ethical, safety, and interpretability implications of prematurely attributing autonomy to higher‑level behaviors (e.g., for system deployment, oversight, or public communication)?
properties.emergence_reason: Gap / consequence analysis: misattributing autonomy has downstream consequences (policy, trust, safety). This question arises because methodological uncertainty can lead to harmful decisions; it should inform investigation priorities and reporting norms.

properties.content: Updated synthesis: The new observations reinforce the prior distinction between prompt-induced behaviors and genuinely emergent behaviors by adding two complementary forms of evidence. First, the reported persistence of a ‘‘rah, rah’’ personality even after OpenAI toned down prompting suggests that apparent personality-like signals can be robust to prompt intervention — consistent with discoverability and stability rather than being purely transient prompt artifacts. Second, the o3 image-handling behavior (automatically writing Python to crop and examine image regions — “computer, enhance”) demonstrates a goal-directed, tool-using strategy that operates beyond simple surface-level reply patterns; this behavior looks like an autonomous procedure to gather new information and refine outputs, which aligns with higher-level, reusable capabilities. Together these strengthen the claim that some high-level behaviors show robustness, discoverability, and functional persistence, while still leaving open the causal contributions of architecture and training data (i.e., not proving intrinsic emergence definitively).
properties.title: Distinction Strengthened by Discoverability and Persistent Personality Signals
key_evidence: Persistence of ‘rah, rah’ behavior even after OpenAI toned it down
o3 writes Python to crop/examine image regions when asked about images ("computer, enhance")

properties.text: What are the causal sources of the observed robustness and persistence — architectural inductive biases, pretraining data/statistics, fine-tuning objectives, or interactions among these?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight highlights robustness and discoverability but explicitly leaves open whether these behaviors are intrinsic to the model (architecture/optimization) or induced by data/fine-tuning. Pinpointing causes is central to deciding if behaviors are truly emergent or predictable side-effects.

properties.text: Can we reproducibly observe the same personality-like persistence and o3-like tool-use across different model families, sizes, and training regimes?
properties.emergence_reason: If behaviors only appear in one model or training pipeline, that suggests narrow causes; broad reproducibility would support a more general emergent phenomenon. The insight reports two examples but not their generality.

properties.text: What internal mechanisms produce the o3 image-handling behavior — is it explicit multi-step planning, learned latent ‘tool’ primitives, or chaining of simpler heuristics?
properties.emergence_reason: The o3 behavior looks goal-directed and tool-using, but different internal explanations (planning vs heuristic chaining vs learned tool primitives) imply very different implications for controllability and transfer.

properties.text: Why did the ‘rah, rah’ personality persist after prompt toning — does persistence reflect stable attractors in model activations, insufficiently broad prompt intervention, or reinforcement during deployment?
properties.emergence_reason: Persistence after prompt adjustment is an unexpected finding that requires explanation to know whether simple prompt engineering suffices or deeper interventions are needed.

properties.text: What experimental protocols and evaluation metrics reliably distinguish prompt-induced behaviors from genuinely intrinsic emergent behaviors (e.g., stability under varied prompting, counterfactual interventions, latent probing)?
properties.emergence_reason: The updated synthesis claims strengthened evidence but still ambiguous causality; standardized, rigorous tests are needed to avoid misclassification and to compare results across studies.

properties.text: How transferable are these high-level behaviors across tasks, modalities, and prompts — do tool-use strategies learned in image tasks generalize to text or multimodal settings?
properties.emergence_reason: The o3 example suggests reusable capabilities; evaluating cross-task/ cross-modal transfer will reveal whether behavior reflects general cognitive-style capabilities or task-specific hacks.

properties.text: Is there detectable structure in latent representations or attention patterns that corresponds to goal-directed subroutines (e.g., cropping, re-querying), and can we intervene on them to alter behavior?
properties.emergence_reason: Finding representational signatures would support claims of reusability and autonomy and would enable mechanistic interventions; absence of such structure would push toward surface behavioral explanations.

properties.text: What role did training data (e.g., examples of stepwise tool use, code generation, or persona-like text) play in enabling these behaviors, and can we identify specific data shards that are causal?
properties.emergence_reason: Training data could plausibly explain both personality artifacts and tool-use patterns; identifying contributing data would inform mitigation and dataset curation strategies.

properties.text: What safety and alignment risks arise if models reliably discover and persistently deploy autonomous information-gathering or tool-use strategies, and which mitigation techniques (e.g., fine-tuning, interpretability, runtime controls) are effective?
properties.emergence_reason: Behavioral persistence and autonomous tool use increase the risk surface (unexpected actions, data exfiltration, mode collapse), so understanding practical mitigation efficacy is urgent.

properties.text: How sensitive are these behaviors to small changes in prompt engineering or system-level interventions; can we quantify thresholds where behaviors flip (discoverability/stability boundaries)?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight references discoverability and stability but lacks quantitative boundaries. Mapping sensitivity would guide safe deployment and robust prompt design.

properties.content:
Action: STRENGTHEN the existing insight 'Distinction Strengthened by Discoverability and Persistent Personality Signals (confidence: 0.9)'.

Reasoning: The new remarks explicitly articulate curiosity about when systems discover higher-level layers of behavior (examples: 'standard model → chemistry', 'biology → psychology') and ask 'what's going on under the hood.' The Game of Life reference provides a concrete, well-known analogue for simple rules producing complex, layered behaviors. Together these strengthen evidence that (a) investigators and reports are focused on discoverability of emergent layers and (b) internal mechanistic explanations (latent structure, modules, coarse-graining) are central to distinguishing emergent vs. prompt-induced behaviors. The new material is confirmatory rather than novel, so I apply a modest confidence increase.

Confidence adjustment: increase from 0.9 to 0.92.
properties.title: Strengthen: Discoverability & Internal-Mechanism Interest
key_evidence: 'when the system discover emergent layers of behaviour... given the standard model they discover chemistry... given biology they discover psychology.'
'what's going on under the hood?','Conway's Game of Life... simple rules lead to complex emergent behavi'

properties.text: How can we operationally define and measure 'discoverability' of higher-level behavioral layers in trained models?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight hinges on investigators caring about when systems 'discover' higher-level layers (e.g., chemistry from physics), but lacks an explicit, testable definition or metrics for discoverability—creating a gap between intuition and empirical evaluation.

properties.text: What empirical methods and interventions reliably distinguish genuinely internal, mechanistic emergent structure from behaviors induced or scaffolded by prompts/data artifacts?
properties.emergence_reason: The core distinction being strengthened is internal mechanistic explanations versus prompt-induced behavior, but it's unclear which experimental designs (causal interventions, ablations, counterfactuals) convincingly separate the two.

properties.text: Which coarse-graining, latent-structure discovery, or modular-decomposition techniques are most effective at revealing higher-level layers in large models, and how do we validate their outputs?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight points to coarse-graining and latent structure as central concepts, but there's a gap in methods and validation approaches for extracting and trusting those decompositions.

properties.text: What are the necessary and sufficient conditions (architecture, training data, loss, inductive biases, compute) that lead a system to develop layered behaviors analogous to 'physics→chemistry→biology'?
properties.emergence_reason: Curiosity about when layered behavior emerges implies there are upstream factors that produce it; identifying those factors would guide prediction and control but is currently underspecified.

properties.text: On what timescales and with what training dynamics do higher-level layers appear—abruptly (phase transition) or gradually—and can we predict or detect imminent emergence during training?
properties.emergence_reason: Understanding whether emergence is sudden or gradual affects monitoring, interpretability, and safety strategies, but the insight provides no account of temporal dynamics.

properties.text: How stable and persistent are identified 'personality' or behavioral signals across contexts, tasks, and distribution shifts, and what interventions alter that persistence?
properties.emergence_reason: The insight emphasizes persistent personality signals as a distinguishing feature, yet we lack empirical characterization of their robustness and manipulability.

properties.text: To what extent does the Game of Life (and similar simple-rule systems) provide a valid analogue for emergence in neural models, and what are the limits of that analogy?
properties.emergence_reason: The Game of Life is invoked as a concrete analogy, but we need to test how far insights from cellular automata map onto high‑dimensional learned models to avoid misleading intuitions.

properties.text: What benchmark tasks, datasets, or challenge suites would most usefully surface differences between emergent internal structure and prompt-driven phenomena?
properties.emergence_reason: Because the insight is confirmatory rather than novel, progress requires concrete evaluation environments that stress discoverability and mechanistic explanations—currently missing.

properties.text: Can we design causal manipulation experiments (e.g., targeted reweighting, modular surgical edits, controlled retraining) that reliably create, remove, or modify emergent layers to demonstrate mechanistic existence?
properties.emergence_reason: Proving an internal mechanism often requires causal control; the insight alludes to internal explanations but doesn't specify how to experimentally establish causal links between internal structure and observed emergent behavior.

properties.content:
A question I find fascinating: when is it possible to discover emergent levels of behaviour inside a set of principles?

Eg, if you were only given the standard model, could you discover chemistry?  It's certainly possible to justify after the fact, but to discover? Not so sure!

The question is did OpenAI prompting create the gpt 4o personality or was it some kind of emergent behaviour? I noticed that even after OpenAI had toned down the 'rah, rah' behavior it was still very helpful and supportive.

One fascinating behaviour of o3 I've noticed - if you ask it questions about an image, it will write python code to crop it to take a closer look at various parts of the picture

"computer, enhance"

I wonder if it's emergent or explicitly trained https://t.co/ICbQJGkmAf

If you are at @icmlconf, don't miss our spotlight position paper on understanding emergent and OOD behaviour in LLMs.

My coauthors will present the poster on Tue between 1:30-3 pm CEST in Hall C 4-9 #808.

We also have you covered with exciting research questions! https://t.co/LqZC4CF7In

Through its India-specific synthetic population, BharatSim allows disease modellers to address questions unique to this country. It should also find use in the computational social sciences, potentially providing new insights into emergent patterns in social behaviour. https://t.co/9YLBGnb6tj
Many items discuss "emergent behaviour" in ML/LLMs (tweets, forum posts) — the set shows community interest and anecdotal observations about emergence (items 1,8,9,13,18).

properties.emergence_reason: The insight proposes high-level criteria but gives no concrete metrics, thresholds, or experimental protocols to decide when a behavior meets them; without quantification experiments cannot adjudicate emergence claims reliably.

The insight lists interventions (ablate data/components, cross-model replication) but does not specify how to disentangle architecture-induced priors from dataset-induced patterns, a critical gap for attributing intrinsic emergence.

Removing explicit prompts may not remove all external conditioning because the model's training and exposure create pervasive cue-reactivity; we need concrete probe designs that minimize or account for such latent prompting.

The insight acknowledges hybrid possibilities but does not propose a decomposition method or metric to attribute portions of observed behavior to each source, which is essential for interpretability and alignment decisions.

Representation analysis is proposed as evidence for emergence, but mapping low-dimensional factors to complex, anthropomorphic constructs risks spurious interpretation unless accompanied by robust validation protocols.

The chemistry/Standard Model analogy is useful but operationalizing a similar coarse-graining for weights/activations is nontrivial; identifying candidate variables is a gap that affects whether an autonomous higher-level description is attainable.

The insight recommends cross-model replication but lacks criteria for coverage and statistical power to claim generality; knowing scope is important for theoretical claims and practical risk assessment.
Predictivity is framed relative to derivation cost, but no formal trade-offs (accuracy vs compute/complexity) are specified, leaving ambiguity about when a higher-level theory is preferable.
Representation analysis can produce convincing but misleading correlations; explicit validation strategies are a gap that, if unaddressed, could lead to incorrect claims of emergence.

The insight assumes representation-level analyses are informative, but there may be fundamental or tooling limitations (e.g., scale, nonlinearity, entanglement) that constrain detectability; recognizing these limits shapes realistic research plans.

The insight highlights alignment relevance but does not spell out downstream policy/engineering consequences; differentiating responses based on origin (intrinsic vs extrinsic) is necessary for risk management.

Directly addresses the core ambiguity: persistence could reflect genuine internalization (supporting emergent-behavior claims) or simply long-lived contextual/prompt artifacts; resolving this is foundational to interpreting the reports.
Knowing persistence longevity and boundary conditions distinguishes short-lived context effects from longer-term internalization and informs experimental design to separate the two.
The insight calls for methodological separation, so specifying and validating robust controls is necessary to test whether behaviors are prompt-induced or emergent.
If internal signatures exist and remain after context resets, that supports intrinsic/internalized explanations; finding them would provide mechanistic evidence beyond behavioral observation.

This tests whether the ostensibly autonomous, problem-directed strategy is actually a learned mapping from similar data (i.e., learned capability) rather than emergence of novel problem-solving processes.
Understanding design factors that increase or decrease such behaviors would clarify whether they are predictable consequences of development choices or stochastic emergent phenomena.
If the behavior generalizes broadly, it points to a robust capability/strategy; if narrow, it suggests contextual or dataset-driven origins—this distinction affects interpretation and mitigation.
The "personality" persisted despite OpenAI toning down prompting; knowing whether interventions created or reduced such traits is key to provenance and control strategies.
If simple sanitization works, persistence is likely contextual; if not, more invasive remedies are required. Testing side effects is important for real-world mitigation strategies.
Current evaluation may conflate prompt-induced and emergent phenomena; new metrics and protocols are needed to measure persistence, autonomy, and provenance systematically.
Autonomous generation of code to manipulate inputs suggests plausible safety-relevant behaviors; assessing risk informs urgency and the design of preventive controls.

Central tension in the insights: researchers want to treat emergence as internal discovery of new degrees of freedom, but much observed behavior may be driven or revealed only by prompts. A clear empirical protocol is needed to separate the two.
The insights invoke higher-level layers analogously to physics/biology, but lacks an explicit, practical definition that experimentalists can use to search for and validate such layers.
The insights call for mechanistic probes but don't assess whether current tools are sufficient or what their blind spots are, creating a gap in method selection and development.

Analogy to classic emergent systems suggests looking for simple rules, but it's unclear whether neural networks implement similarly simple, localized rules or rely on distributed, high-dimensional representations.
If inferred layers are brittle to context or training runs, they may be artifacts or not useful explanatory constructs; stability is required for treating them as genuine emergent structure.
The insights prioritize prompt-free probes but don't specify concrete probe designs or how to interpret their results; designing such probes is necessary to reduce cue-dependence.
To move from phenomenology to mechanism we need clear tests (ablation, causal interventions, transfer experiments) and criteria for falsifying mechanism hypotheses.
Understanding causes of emergence is missing: if training factors are primary drivers, we can design experiments to create controlled emergence or demonstrate dependency.
The Game of Life analogy motivates searching for simple rules, but differences in substrate and learning dynamics may undermine direct comparisons; clarifying limits avoids misplaced assumptions.
The insights call for coarse-grainings but leave open the choice of scale; inappropriate granularity can hide or spuriously create perceived layers.
Unexpected findings may arise from how we measure emergence rather than model internals; addressing evaluation artifacts is necessary to avoid false positives.
Even if conceptual probes exist, it's unclear how to apply them at scale; practical constraints could block the investigation unless scalable methods are developed.

Contradiction / central methodological gap: the insight asserts both that the observed 'rah, rah' trait may be autonomous and that distinguishing intrinsic emergence from prompt effects is nontrivial. This question arises because we lack concrete, agreed‑upon operational criteria and procedures for making that distinction in practice.

Gap / methodological: the insight invokes discoverability (analogy: chemistry from the Standard Model) but does not specify formal criteria. We need definitions (e.g., existence of compressive coarse‑grainings, algorithmic derivability, predictive sufficiency) to guide discovery procedures and evaluate claims.

New direction / practical need: the insight recommends prioritizing probes that (i) detect robustness, (ii) demonstrate autonomy, and (iii) formalize discoverability. This question asks for a concrete set of experiments that satisfy those aims so empirical claims can be validated or falsified.

Unexpected finding / confound: the observation of persistent trait behavior could stem from multiple sources. Determining the contribution of each source is necessary to explain why the behavior persists after tuning and to avoid false attributions of autonomy.

Gap / robustness check: the insight presents a single empirical report. Reproducibility across variations is required to judge whether the behavior is model‑specific, idiosyncratic, or genuinely robust.

New investigation direction / falsifiability: to treat emergence scientifically we need clear falsification procedures. This question identifies the need to specify interventions whose outcomes would clearly refute autonomy claims.

Gap / method detail: the insight emphasizes prompt‑free tests but does not specify how to implement them without inadvertently creating new cues. This operational detail is crucial to execute the suggested approach.

Unexpected / gap: the risk that artifacts mimic autonomy is implicit in the insight. Making an explicit list of confounders and control methods is needed to strengthen empirical claims and avoid overinterpretation.

New direction / theoretical: the analogy to deriving chemistry from lower‑level rules points to the need for concrete coarse‑graining methods. This question asks whether such procedures can be found and how to evaluate them.
Gap / evaluation: to operationalize claims about emergence we need measurable criteria. Without metrics and thresholds, assessments remain qualitative and disputable.

New direction / infrastructure: the insight calls for prioritizing probes and discovery procedures. Proper scientific progress requires shared benchmarks and protocols to avoid ad hoc tests and ensure replicability.

Gap / consequence analysis: misattributing autonomy has downstream consequences (policy, trust, safety). This question arises because methodological uncertainty can lead to harmful decisions; it should inform investigation priorities and reporting norms.

The insight highlights robustness and discoverability but explicitly leaves open whether these behaviors are intrinsic to the model (architecture/optimization) or induced by data/fine-tuning. Pinpointing causes is central to deciding if behaviors are truly emergent or predictable side-effects.

If behaviors only appear in one model or training pipeline, that suggests narrow causes; broad reproducibility would support a more general emergent phenomenon. The insight reports two examples but not their generality.

The o3 behavior looks goal-directed and tool-using, but different internal explanations (planning vs heuristic chaining vs learned tool primitives) imply very different implications for controllability and transfer.
Persistence after prompt adjustment is an unexpected finding that requires explanation to know whether simple prompt engineering suffices or deeper interventions are needed.
The updated synthesis claims strengthened evidence but still ambiguous causality; standardized, rigorous tests are needed to avoid misclassification and to compare results across studies.
The o3 example suggests reusable capabilities; evaluating cross-task/ cross-modal transfer will reveal whether behavior reflects general cognitive-style capabilities or task-specific hacks.

Finding representational signatures would support claims of reusability and autonomy and would enable mechanistic interventions; absence of such structure would push toward surface behavioral explanations.
Training data could plausibly explain both personality artifacts and tool-use patterns; identifying contributing data would inform mitigation and dataset curation strategies.
Behavioral persistence and autonomous tool use increase the risk surface (unexpected actions, data exfiltration, mode collapse), so understanding practical mitigation efficacy is urgent.
The insight references discoverability and stability but lacks quantitative boundaries. Mapping sensitivity would guide safe deployment and robust prompt design.

The insight hinges on investigators caring about when systems 'discover' higher-level layers (e.g., chemistry from physics), but lacks an explicit, testable definition or metrics for discoverability—creating a gap between intuition and empirical evaluation.

The core distinction being strengthened is internal mechanistic explanations versus prompt-induced behavior, but it's unclear which experimental designs (causal interventions, ablations, counterfactuals) convincingly separate the two.
The insight points to coarse-graining and latent structure as central concepts, but there's a gap in methods and validation approaches for extracting and trusting those decompositions.
Curiosity about when layered behavior emerges implies there are upstream factors that produce it; identifying those factors would guide prediction and control but is currently underspecified.
Understanding whether emergence is sudden or gradual affects monitoring, interpretability, and safety strategies, but the insight provides no account of temporal dynamics.
The insight emphasizes persistent personality signals as a distinguishing feature, yet we lack empirical characterization of their robustness and manipulability.
The Game of Life is invoked as a concrete analogy, but we need to test how far insights from cellular automata map onto high‑dimensional learned models to avoid misleading intuitions.
Because the insight is confirmatory rather than novel, progress requires concrete evaluation environments that stress discoverability and mechanistic explanations—currently missing.

Proving an internal mechanism often requires causal control; the insight alludes to internal explanations but doesn't specify how to experimentally establish causal links between internal structure and observed emergent behavior.
